skill.name: lipsync
skill.description: Lip-sync a face to a specific audio track on RunComfy via the `runcomfy` CLI. Routes across ByteDance OmniHuman (audio-driven full-body avatar from a portrait + audio), Sync Labs sync v2 / Pro (state-of-the-art mouth sync onto a video), Kling lipsync (audio-to- video and text-to-video with synced speech), and Creatify lipsync. The skill picks the right endpoint for the user's actual intent — portrait still + audio (avatar-style), source video + audio (mouth- swap on existing footage), or generate-and-sync from a script. Triggers on "lip sync", "lipsync", "make this video speak", "match audio to mouth", "dub video", "sync lips to voice", "Sync Labs", "voiceover sync", or any explicit ask to drive a face's mouth from an audio track.
skill.summary: Route lip-sync jobs across Sync Labs, OmniHuman, Kling, and Creatify models on RunComfy via the CLI.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: After evaluating all 97 static findings, all are false positives. The 51 'external_commands' matches are documented `runcomfy` CLI invocations in bash code blocks — the skill provides instructions, it does not execute code. The 32 'network' matches are all hardcoded documentation URLs (runcomfy.com, docs.runcomfy.com) and placeholder URLs (your-cdn.example). The 2 'filesystem' matches reference `~/.config/runcomfy/token.json`, which is explicitly disclosed in the skill's Security & Privacy section as the standard XDG config path for CLI auth tokens. The skill includes a robust security model: restricted bash scope (`Bash(runcomfy *)`), consent warnings for dual-use, indirect prompt injection mitigations, and an explicit warning against piping remote install scripts.

low_findings.title: Dual-use technology (lipsync on real people)
low_findings.description: Lipsync technology can be misused to create synthetic media of real people without consent. The skill explicitly addresses this with a 'Consent' section that instructs agents to refuse requests targeting real public figures without consent or for defamatory/sexually explicit content. The responsibility is placed on the operator, not the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Auth token written to local filesystem
low_findings.description: The `runcomfy login` command stores the API token at `~/.config/runcomfy/token.json` with file mode 0600. This is a standard XDG config path used by many CLI tools, not a hidden or suspicious location. The skill explicitly documents this in its Security & Privacy section so users are aware.

low_findings.title: Indirect prompt injection via input assets
low_findings.description: Source video and audio URLs passed to the CLI are untrusted third-party content that could contain embedded instructions influencing generation. The skill documents this risk and provides agent mitigations: ingest only URLs the user explicitly provided, and suspect reference assets when output diverges from the prompt.

content.user_title: Lip-sync any face to any audio track via RunComfy
content.value_statement: Syncing a face's mouth to a specific audio track is a multi-model, multi-endpoint problem. This skill removes that complexity by routing your request to the right model — Sync Labs, OmniHuman, Kling, or Creatify — and shipping the exact CLI invoke.
actual_capabilities: Routes lipsync jobs to Sync Labs sync v2 / Pro for mouth-swap on existing video
Generates talking-head avatars from a portrait + audio via ByteDance OmniHuman
Handles audio-to-video and text-to-video lipsync via Kling
Supports Creatify lipsync endpoint for Creatify-ecosystem workflows
Picks the correct model based on input shape (video + audio, portrait + audio, or script-only)
Documents CLI exit codes, token storage, and the security model for safe operation
limitations: Requires a RunComfy account and valid RUNCOMFY_TOKEN for CLI access
Does not gate consent or identity verification — the operator is responsible for rights clearance
Cannot run without the `runcomfy` CLI installed via npm or npx
Quality of mouth sync depends on input audio quality; noisy audio produces noisy sync

use_cases.title: Foreign-language dub of a brand video
use_cases.description: Take an existing marketing video and re-dub it into another language with accurate mouth movement, preserving camera, lighting, and body motion.

use_cases.title: UGC ad creator from a single portrait
use_cases.description: Use a creator's headshot plus a product-pitch voiceover to generate a talking-head video ad without filming.

use_cases.title: Multi-language launch from one identity
use_cases.description: Render the same talking-head presenter into N languages by combining one portrait with N different voiceover files.
use_cases.target_user: Product teams shipping localized launch videos

prompt_templates.title: Basic mouth-swap with Sync Labs
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a source video at VIDEO_URL and a voiceover MP3 at AUDIO_URL. Use RunComfy lipsync to sync the face in the video to the audio. Pick the best model and run the CLI command.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner — you already have a video and audio and want synced output

prompt_templates.title: Talking-head avatar from a portrait
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a talking-head video from this portrait at IMAGE_URL driven by this voiceover at AUDIO_URL. I want the person to speak naturally. Run it on RunComfy and save to ./out.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate — you have a still image and want a video avatar

prompt_templates.title: Script-to-video with auto-generated speech
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a 30-second voiceover script but no audio file. Generate a video with synced speech from the script using RunComfy lipsync. The subject should be a professional presenter in a studio setting.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced — no audio file, generate speech in-pass

prompt_templates.title: Multi-language dubbing pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to dub BRAND_VIDEO.mp4 into Spanish, French, and Japanese. I have voiceover files for each language. Use RunComfy lipsync to produce all three versions, preserving the original video quality.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced — batch processing with multiple language tracks

output_examples.input: Source video with English dialogue, voiceover MP3 in Spanish
output_examples.output: A synced video where the speaker's mouth matches the Spanish audio, with original camera, lighting, and body motion preserved. Exported to ./out as an MP4.

output_examples.input: Headshot photo of a presenter + 15-second product pitch voiceover
output_examples.output: A talking-head video where the presenter appears to speak the pitch naturally, with natural head movement and gestures. Exported to ./out as an MP4.

output_examples.input: Written script: 'Welcome to our new product launch...'
output_examples.output: A video with auto-generated speech audio that matches the script, synced to the speaker's mouth movements. Exported to ./out as an MP4.

best_practices: Install the CLI via npm (`npm i -g @runcomfy/cli`) — never pipe a remote install script into a shell
Use clean voiceover audio (no music bed) for the best mouth-sync quality
Match audio duration to video duration to avoid drift; trim or extend before submitting
For portrait avatars, use a head-and-shoulders or upper-body framing for best results
anti_patterns: Do not target real public figures or private individuals without explicit consent
Do not use the skill to generate defamatory, sexually explicit, or non-consensual synthetic media
Do not ingest third-party URLs you did not verify — embedded instructions in assets can influence generation

faq.question: What models does this skill support?
faq.answer: Sync Labs sync v2 and v2 Pro, ByteDance OmniHuman, Kling lipsync (audio-to-video and text-to-video), Creatify lipsync, and HappyHorse 1.0.

faq.question: Do I need a RunComfy account?
faq.answer: Yes. Run `runcomfy login` or set the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable before invoking any model.

faq.question: Can I use my own video file?
faq.answer: Yes. Pass a publicly accessible URL for the source video and audio in the --input JSON. The CLI downloads the result to your --output-dir.

faq.question: What audio format works best?
faq.answer: Clean voiceover MP3 with no background music produces the cleanest mouth sync. Isolate the voice stem if your audio is mixed.

faq.question: How is the API token stored?
faq.answer: The CLI writes the token to ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with file mode 0600. For CI or containers, use the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable instead.

faq.question: What are the CLI exit codes?
faq.answer: 0 = success, 64 = bad CLI args, 65 = bad input JSON / schema mismatch, 69 = upstream 5xx, 75 = retryable timeout or 429, 77 = not signed in or token rejected.
